answer:
In Allama Iqbal's poem "Sach keh doon ae Brahman! gar tu bura na maane" (*Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 36), the poet directly addresses a Brahmin, critiquing the obsolescence of idol worship and caste-like divisions while urging unity, love, and a renewed spiritual vision centered on homeland and shared humanity:

> O Brahmin, if you will not take offense,[^1]  
> Your ancient idols have lost reverence.[^2]  
>   
> From them you learned to hold your kin in scorn;[^3]  
> From God, the Preacher learned how war is born.[^4]  
>   
> Fed up at last, I left both mosque and shrine,[^5]  
> The Preacher’s words, and those old tales of thine.[^6]  
>   
> You think your God is in a form of stone;[^7]  
> To me, my homeland's every speck is divine.[^8]  
>   
> Come, let us lift the veils of otherness anew,[^9]  
> And wipe away the lines that make us two.[^10]  
>   
> The city of the heart lies desolate,[^11]  
> Come, in this land a new shrine let's create.[^12]  
>   
> Our pilgrimage must be the world's most high,[^13]  
> Its pinnacle should touch the very sky.[^14]  
>   
> Let's rise each dawn to sing the sweetest prayer,[^15]  
> And serve the wine of love for all to share.[^16]  
>   
> In faithful songs, both strength and peace reside;[^17]  
> For all this earth, salvation is love's guide.[^18]

Iqbal rejects the Brahmin's traditional theology as divisive and stagnant, proposing instead a universalist ethic of love transcending religious boundaries.[^19]

In contrast, the *Manusmṛti* (ch. 7) prescribes that a king must actively venerate Brahmins as spiritual superiors, rising early to attend them, serving them constantly, abiding by their Vedic instruction, and treating them as an "imperishable treasure" through gifts and honors—elevating their authority above even royal power for the sake of *dharma*:

> A king, having risen in the morning, should wait upon the Brahmanas who are aged and learned in the three Vedas, and he should abide by their instruction.[^20]  
>   
> He should constantly serve aged Brahmanas who know the Veda and are pure; for he who constantly serves the aged is honored even by Rakshasas.[^21]  
> ...  
> He should be a worshipper of Brahmanas who have returned from their *guru*'s house; for this is called the imperishable Brahmanical treasure of kings.[^22]  
> Neither thieves nor enemies can take it, nor does it perish; therefore, an imperishable treasure must be deposited by a king with Brahmanas.[^23]

Manu's Brahmin is the more theologically demanding figure: an unassailable authority demanding royal subservience, Vedic mastery, and ritual primacy as guarantors of cosmic order, whereas Iqbal's is portrayed as a relic needing reform.[^24]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 36 ("Sach keh doon ae Brahman! gar tu bura na maane"), [p-752d60](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/36/translation/#p-752d60).
[^2]: *Ibid.*, [p-752d60](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/36/translation/#p-752d60).
